title: The Nervous System and Sleep: Why Trauma Disrupts Rest
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts your nervous system and disrupts sleep, and learn somatic strategies to reclaim restful nights.
body:
Introduction: The Hidden Connection Between Trauma and Sleep

For many who have experienced trauma, sleep is elusive. You might lie awake, heart racing, mind spinning, or find yourself waking repeatedly, drenched in sweat or overwhelmed by panic. This is not just a side effect of stress — it’s your nervous system locked in survival mode, sabotaging rest. Understanding how trauma disrupts sleep means looking deeply at the nervous system’s role and how it governs safety, arousal, and rest.

Trauma leaves imprints on your body’s regulatory systems. As Bessel van der Kolk emphasizes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma is stored in the body and nervous system, not just the mind. Sleep disturbance is a key symptom of this biological imprint, and healing requires working with the body, not just the brain.

The Nervous System’s Role in Sleep Regulation

The autonomic nervous system (ANS) controls involuntary functions like heart rate, digestion, and, critically, sleep-wake cycles. It has two main branches:

  • Sympathetic Nervous System (SNS): Activates fight, flight, or freeze responses — increasing alertness and preparing the body to respond to threats.
  • Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS): Promotes rest, digestion, and repair, calming the body down and facilitating restorative sleep.

Sleep requires a shift from sympathetic dominance to parasympathetic activation. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the vagus nerve, a major PNS component, helps downregulate arousal and promote a sense of safety necessary for deep sleep. When trauma disrupts this balance, the nervous system remains hypervigilant, making it hard to fall or stay asleep (Porges, 2011).

How Trauma Hijacks Your Nervous System and Sleep

Trauma triggers chronic activation of the SNS and dysregulation of the vagal brake — the mechanism that slows heart rate and calms the body. This leads to hyperarousal, a state where the body is constantly on edge. As a result, the brain's ability to transition into deep, restorative sleep stages is impaired.

Research by Peter Levine and Pat Ogden highlights that trauma keeps the body stuck in a defensive state, preventing the natural downshifts needed for sleep. The nervous system anticipates danger, so even if the environment is safe, the body won’t relax fully (Levine, 1997; Ogden et al., 2006).

Nightmares and Night Terrors: The Body’s Alarm System

Nightmares and night terrors often reflect the nervous system’s unresolved trauma responses. Francine Shapiro, founder of EMDR therapy, notes that these disruptive dreams are the brain’s attempt to process trauma but can backfire, increasing fear and arousal instead of resolving it (Shapiro, 2018).

Increased norepinephrine and cortisol (stress hormones) during sleep further fragment rest, leading to less time in slow-wave and REM sleep — the most restorative phases crucial for memory consolidation and emotional regulation (van der Kolk, 2014).

Why Traditional Therapies Often Fall Short

Many who have tried talk therapy still struggle with sleep because trauma is stored in the body, not just the narrative. Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology reminds us that integration of body and mind is essential for healing. Without addressing somatic symptoms and nervous system dysregulation, trauma symptoms persist, including sleep problems (Siegel, 2012).

Gabor Maté emphasizes that trauma-informed healing involves understanding how early adverse experiences shape nervous system development, influencing sleep patterns decades later (Maté, 2018). This is why somatic-based approaches are fundamental to reclaiming rest.

Somatic Tools to Restore Nervous System Balance and Sleep

Regulating the nervous system is the foundation for improving sleep after trauma. Here are evidence-based somatic strategies:

  • Polyvagal-Informed Breathwork: Slow, diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the vagus nerve, activating the parasympathetic system and reducing arousal (Porges, 2017).
  • Body Awareness and Grounding: Techniques like body scans or mindful movement help reconnect you with your physical sensations, fostering safety and presence (Ogden et al., 2006).
  • Somatic Experiencing®: Developed by Peter Levine, this method helps release trauma energy by tracking physical sensations and allowing the nervous system to complete defensive responses (Levine, 1997).
  • Safe Sleep Environment: Adjust lighting, noise, and bedding to support parasympathetic activation. Consistent routines signal safety to the nervous system (van der Kolk, 2014).
  • Progressive Muscle Relaxation: Systematic tensing and relaxing of muscle groups lowers sympathetic activation and promotes deep relaxation.

Integrating the Parts: Using Internal Family Systems for Sleep

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model offers a framework to work with the internal voices or parts that may resist sleep due to trauma-based fear. These parts often keep you awake as a protective measure.

By acknowledging and gently negotiating with these parts, you can reduce internal conflict and nervous system hyperarousal. This can open the door for deeper rest and integration (Schwartz, 2013).

When to Seek Professional Support

If sleep disturbances are severe or persistent, it’s important to reach out for trauma-informed professional help. Somatic therapists trained in trauma resolution, EMDR, or polyvagal-informed approaches can provide tailored interventions.

Remember, healing is a process. As Francine Shapiro reminds us, addressing trauma at the nervous system level requires patience and compassionate self-care (Shapiro, 2018).

"Trauma is not just an event that took place long ago; it’s also the imprint left on mind, brain, and body." – Bessel van der Kolk

Conclusion: Restoring Rest Through Nervous System Healing

Sleep disruption after trauma is not simply a symptom to be medicated away — it is a sign that your nervous system remains stuck in survival mode. Healing sleep means healing the nervous system through body-based, somatic practices that restore safety and regulation.

By understanding the science behind trauma’s impact on sleep and actively engaging in somatic tools, you reclaim not just rest, but your capacity for resilience and well-being.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  • Levine, P. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. W. (2017). "The Pocket Guide to the Polyvagal Theory." W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (2013). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: Why does trauma cause sleep disturbances?
faqs.a: Trauma keeps the nervous system in a state of hyperarousal, making it difficult to shift into the restful parasympathetic state necessary for quality sleep.

faqs.q: Can somatic practices really improve sleep after trauma?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic practices help regulate the nervous system by promoting safety and calming physiological arousal, which supports falling and staying asleep.

faqs.q: How is the vagus nerve connected to sleep?
faqs.a: The vagus nerve activates the parasympathetic nervous system, helping to slow heart rate and promote relaxation necessary for restorative sleep.

faqs.q: When should I seek professional help for trauma-related sleep issues?
faqs.a: If sleep disturbances are severe, persistent, or significantly impair daily functioning, seeking trauma-informed professional support is recommended.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and nervous system, essential for understanding trauma and sleep disruption.

metaTitle: Trauma, Nervous System & Sleep: Why Rest Is Disrupted
metaDescription: Discover why trauma disrupts sleep through nervous system dysregulation and learn somatic strategies to restore restful nights.
author: The Oracle Lover
